title: tick() spreads entire jobs Map into an array on every RAF frame
description: Every tick() call does `[...this.jobs.values()]` which allocates a new array of all registered jobs, then chains .filter() x3 and .map() x2. With maxQueuedJobs up to 50 this happens on every animation frame (60fps) even when nothing is eligible. The intermediate arrays from each chained filter/map are all short-lived garbage. Pre-filtering to only eligible jobs or caching the sorted candidate list until job state changes would eliminate the repeated alloc/GC pressure.
code_excerpt:
const candidates = [...this.jobs.values()]
      .filter((j) => j.visible || j.nearViewport || j.selected)
      .filter((j) => tierRank(j.currentTier) < tierRank(j.targetTier))
      .filter((j) => j.decoderAbort === null)
      .filter((j) => !j.nextRetryAt || now >= j.nextRetryAt)
      .map((j) => ({ job: j, score: fairnessScore(j, now) }))
      .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score)
      .map((p) => p.job);

title: tierRank() called 4 times per job per tick (two filters × two jobs in comparison)
description: Each filter pass calls `tierRank(j.currentTier)` and `tierRank(j.targetTier)` per job. tierRank performs a object-literal lookup each call. With 50 jobs this is 200 redundant lookups per tick. The sort comparator for fairnessScore also calls tierRank internally (underRefinedBonus = 3 - tierRank). Since tier values only change on explicit transitions, these could be precomputed once per job or inlined as numeric fields.

title: armEarliestRetryTimer iterates all jobs on every tick even when no jobs are retrying
description: armEarliestRetryTimer is called unconditionally at the start of every tick(). It iterates the entire jobs Map to find the minimum nextRetryAt. When no jobs are in error state (the common case), this is a pure waste. A simple errorJobCount counter, or maintaining a separate errored-jobs Set, would let this function short-circuit in O(1) for the common case.

title: onChunk allocates a new Uint8Array wrapper for every incoming fetch chunk
description: The onChunk capture callback does `const chunk = new Uint8Array(c)` on every chunk received from the tee stream, wrapping a Uint8Array that is already a Uint8Array. This is a zero-copy-intent path (the tee branch exists specifically to avoid re-allocation), but the extra wrapper allocation is pointless since `c` is already a Uint8Array. Removing the wrapper (`const chunk = c`) keeps identical semantics with no allocation.

title: New TextDecoder instance allocated on every getManifest cache hit
description: getManifest creates `new TextDecoder()` on every call. This is called for every image in the gallery on each tier upgrade and on prefetch. A module-level singleton `const UTF8_DECODER = new TextDecoder()` would eliminate repeated object construction and the associated hidden class allocation on the V8 fast path.

title: checkHash converts SHA-256 digest to hex via Array.from + map + join — O(32) but needlessly expensive
description: Both checkHash (progressive-manifest.ts:218-223) and computeSha256 (progressive-profile.ts:34-38) independently build the hex string by calling `Array.from(new Uint8Array(hashBuf))` which allocates a 32-element JS array, then `.map()` which allocates another 32-element array of strings, then `.join('')`. A single typed-array loop writing directly into a pre-allocated string avoids the three allocations. This runs once per full-tier verify and once per profileJxl call, so the impact is modest but the fix is trivial and both call-sites share the same pattern.

title: fullPrefix slice + buffer slice allocates two extra copies of the tier bytes at persist time
description: After capture.settled(), the persist path does `job.prefixAccum.slice(0, job.prefixBytes)` which allocates a new Uint8Array, then `fullPrefix.buffer.slice(fullPrefix.byteOffset, ...)` which allocates a new ArrayBuffer. For large tiers (preview up to 70% of file, full = 100%) this is a costly duplicate allocation. Since prefixAccum is already an owned buffer with a known logical length (prefixBytes), passing `prefixAccum.buffer`, `prefixAccum.byteOffset`, and `prefixBytes` directly to setByteRange (or using a subarray view) would avoid one allocation; the byteOffset slice is still needed for correct OPFS writes but the intermediate Uint8Array copy is not.

title: evictBitmaps rebuilds a Set of keep-prefixes on every call
description: evictBitmaps maps the exceptJxlUrls array to full prefixed keys with `new Set(exceptJxlUrls.map(u => BITMAP_KEY_PREFIX + u))` on every invocation. If the caller passes the same URL list repeatedly (e.g. current viewport), the Set is rebuilt from scratch. This is bounded by gallery size but the redundant string concatenations per-URL per-call are avoidable if the caller caches the Set or if the method accepts a pre-built Set.

title: invalidate() iterates bitmapStore via spread of keys() into a temporary array
description: `invalidate` does `[...this.bitmapStore.keys()]` to copy all keys before iterating, because deleting from a Map while iterating its keys() is safe in JS (the spec guarantees it). The spread is unnecessary — Map.prototype.keys() is safe to iterate while deleting entries visited later. The copy doubles memory for stores with many bitmap entries.

title: onChunk throttle check calls Date.now() for every received network chunk
description: Inside onChunk (the hot tee capture path), `const now = Date.now()` is evaluated on every incoming chunk to implement a 50ms progress throttle. Network streaming of a large JXL file can produce hundreds of chunks. Date.now() is a syscall and while cheap, it is called unconditionally even when the throttle would not fire. A coarser approach (e.g. checking only every Nth chunk) or using performance.now() which is cached by the engine would reduce overhead on high-throughput streams.

title: concatUint8Arrays filters with instanceof checks on every element even when called with a known-valid array
description: concatUint8Arrays begins with `.filter((c): c is Uint8Array => c instanceof Uint8Array && c.byteLength > 0)`, performing a typeof/instanceof test on every element. This function is only called from within the package with arrays of Uint8Arrays (no mixed types), so the guard is defensive overhead. Additionally the `filter` allocates a new array before the reduce and the set loop. Given callers already control the inputs, the filter could be removed or replaced with a lightweight debug assertion.

title: selectTiers allocates a filtered array (before70) even when the result would be the same element as dcEvent
description: selectTiers calls `events.filter(e => e.byteOffset < totalBytes * 0.7)` and takes `before70[before70.length - 1]`. This allocates a filtered array of potentially all events just to get the last qualifying element. A single reverse-scan loop (scanning from the end of events backward) would find the same element in O(n) without the intermediate array allocation.

title: No benchmark or regression test for scheduler throughput under maxQueuedJobs load
description: There are no benchmarks measuring tick() time with a full 50-job queue, onChunk throughput under simulated streaming, or armEarliestRetryTimer cost. Without a perf baseline, the per-RAF allocation patterns (findings performance-1, performance-2, performance-3) can silently regress as the job set grows. A simple microbenchmark with a populated gallery calling tick() 1000 times would establish a defensible threshold.
